g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint from the Components sheet for organizing the assembly's hardware.
The Visual: A white rounded rectangle on a blue background serves as a designated sorting area. Inside is a silhouette of a screw with a measurement marker indicating a length of 3/8". Below the silhouette is a white square icon with the letter "A".
Text Prompt: "#4-40 Screw x 6".
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Screw Identification: This box is specifically for the Type A screws. At 3/8" long, these are the mid-length screws in your kit. To be sure you have the right ones, you can physically lay one of your screws over the silhouette on the sheet; the threads should match the length of the drawing exactly.
Quantity Check: You should have a total of 6 of these screws. These are primarily used for attaching the legs to the servo horns. If you have extra or are missing some, double-check your Type C (3/4") and Type D (1/4") piles, as it's easy to mix them up at a glance.
Threading Inspection: Before you move to the assembly phase, quickly roll the screws between your fingers. The threads should be clean and smooth. If you find one that is difficult to turn into a nut later, it might have a tiny bit of debris in the threads that can be wiped away now.
g.title: #4-40 3/8-inch screws

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint from the Components sheet for organizing the robot's secondary hardware.
The Visual: A white rounded rectangle on a blue background serves as a designated sorting area for nuts. Inside is a 3D isometric silhouette of a hexagonal nut. Unlike the screw sections, there is no letter icon in this box.
Text Prompt: "#4-40 Nut x 10".
Tactile & Mechanical Tips The "Trap" Hardware: These are the #4-40 Hex Nuts. They are the matching partners for the Type A, C, and D screws you’ve already sorted. You should have exactly 10 of them. Because they are the smallest metal parts in the kit, they are the most prone to rolling away; keeping them grouped in this box is critical.
Nut Orientation: While these nuts are generally symmetrical, check the faces. Sometimes one side is slightly flatter than the other. When sliding these into the "nut traps" in the wooden legs or the torso, ensure they are seated perfectly flat. If a nut is tilted, the screw will not be able to catch the threads.
Testing the Fit: If you are unsure if you have the right hardware, try spinning one nut onto the end of a Type C screw. It should spin on smoothly with your fingers. If it feels stuck or "gritty," check the nut for any metal burrs or debris before placing it back on the sheet.
g.title: #4-40 hex nuts

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint from the Components sheet for organizing the robot's mechanical linkages.
The Visual: A white-outlined silhouette of a Servo Horn is centered on a blue background. The part is a long, symmetrical wooden strip with a circular center, two small screw holes on either side of the center, and notched "T-shaped" ends.
Text Prompt: "Servo Horn x 2".
Tactile & Mechanical Tips The "Pivot Point": You should have 2 of these wooden Servo Horns. These are the critical parts that translate the circular motion of the motors into the walking motion of the legs. When placing them on the sheet, ensure the center circular cutout is clear of any plywood "slugs" so it can fit snugly onto the motor's spline later.
g.title: Wood Pieces - Servo Horns

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint from the Components sheet for organizing the robot's wooden motor mounts.
The Visual: A white rounded rectangle on a blue background serves as a designated sorting area. Inside is a silhouette of the Servo Holder, a small, rectangular plywood piece with two circular screw holes and two rectangular tabs projecting from the sides.
Text Prompt: "Servo Holder x 2".
Tactile & Mechanical Tips The "Clamp" Component: You should have exactly 2 of these holders. Their job is to sandwich the FS90 Servo Motors against the main body of the robot, acting as a structural bracket.
Orientation and Tabs: When placing these on the sheet, note the two tabs on the sides. These tabs are designed to lock into the slots on the Torso and Trunk pieces. Ensure the tabs aren't chipped or rounded off, as a tight fit here is what keeps your robot's motors from wobbling during a walk.
Screw Hole Alignment: The two circular holes in the holder align perfectly with the mounting "ears" on the servo motors. You’ll be passing the long 3/4" #4-40 Screws (C) through these holes. Before assembly, it’s a good idea to ensure no laser-cut debris is blocking these holes so the screws can pass through smoothly.
g.title: Wood Pieces - Servo Holder

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint from the Components sheet for organizing the robot's long hardware.
The Visual: A white rounded rectangle on a blue background serves as a sorting area for "Type C" hardware. Inside is a silhouette of a long screw with a measurement marker indicating a length of 3/4". Below the silhouette is a white square icon with the letter "C".
Text Prompt: "#4-40 Screw x 4".
Tactile & Mechanical Tips The "Long" Screws: These are the Type C screws, the longest ones in your kit. Because they are 3/4" long, they are twice the length of the Type A screws you just organized. You can easily tell them apart by their length—these are designed to pass through the Torso, the Servo Motor, and the Servo Holder all at once.
Quantity Check: You should have a total of 4 of these screws. They are specifically used in pairs for mounting the two servo motors to the robot's main frame.
Avoiding Cross-Threading: Because these screws are long, it’s important to ensure they are perfectly straight when you start threading them into a nut during assembly. If you feel resistance early on, back the screw out and try again to avoid damaging the threads or the wooden frame.
g.title: #4-40 3/4-inch screws

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint from the Components sheet for organizing the robot's leg attachments.
The Visual: A white rounded rectangle on a blue background serves as a designated sorting area for the grommets. Inside is a 3D isometric silhouette of a spool-shaped Grommet.
Text Prompt: "Grommet x 4".
Tactile & Mechanical Tips The "Shoes" of the Robot: You should have exactly 4 of these grommets—one for each leg. These are usually made of rubber or soft plastic and act as the "feet" for your Walker robot, providing the grip it needs to move across a surface.
Cleaning and Traction: Since these parts make direct contact with the ground, they can pick up dust or hair, which causes the robot to slip. Before placing them on the sheet (or before re-assembling), wipe the outer "tread" of the grommet with your thumb to ensure it feels tacky and clean.
Shape Inspection: Ensure each grommet is perfectly circular and hasn't been squashed or deformed. If they were previously installed, check the center "channel" for any wooden splinters from the legs that might prevent them from seating flush next time.
g.title: Rubber Grommets

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint from the Components sheet for organizing the robot's plastic motor attachments.
The Visual: A white rounded rectangle on a blue background serves as a designated sorting area for the spline horns. Inside is a 3D isometric silhouette of a plastic Spline Horn, which has a central geared socket (the spline) and two cylindrical mounting pillars. A small exclamation point icon is on the left edge.
Text Prompt: "Spline Horn x 2" and a warning: "Do not remove once attached."
Tactile & Mechanical Tips The "Friction Fit": You should have 2 of these plastic Spline Horns. These are the parts that press directly onto the metal or plastic gear (the spline) of the FS90 Servo Motor. They are designed for a very tight fit to ensure there is no "slip" when the motor turns.
The Warning Label: The note "Do not remove once attached" is important! These parts use a one-way friction fit that can weaken if they are repeatedly pulled on and off. During disassembly, if these are already screwed onto your wooden Servo Horns, it is often better to leave the plastic and wood joined together to prevent wearing out the screw holes.
Spline Alignment: Look closely at the center hole of the plastic part; you will see tiny "teeth" or ridges. When you eventually reassemble the robot, these must align with the teeth on the motor shaft. If they don't slide on with firm thumb pressure, don't force them—rotate the horn slightly until the teeth line up.
Cleaning the Gears: Use a dry toothbrush or a puff of air to ensure no sawdust from the plywood has settled into the geared center of the horn. Even a tiny piece of debris can prevent the horn from seating fully on the motor.
g.title: Plastic Pieces - Spline Horns

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint from the Components sheet showing the outlines for the robot's legs and spines.
The Visual: Two white-outlined shapes are shown against a blue background, serving as a placement guide for organizing parts. In the upper left is the silhouette of a Leg, and to the right is the larger, elongated silhouette of a Spine angled diagonally.
Text Prompt: "Legs x 4" and "Spine x 2".
Tactile & Mechanical Tips The "Knolling" Process: This sheet is designed for "knolling"—the process of organizing your parts in parallel or 90-degree angles. Placing your laser-cut plywood pieces exactly over these outlines helps you quickly account for all 4 legs and both spines, ensuring nothing was left behind on the robot or the floor.
Symmetry Check: While all four legs look similar, check the etched markings on your physical parts. Placing them on this sheet is a great time to ensure they are all clean and that the "nut trap" slots at the top are clear of any leftover wooden debris.
Spine Alignment: The spines are the largest structural connectors. When you lay them down, ensure the small circular hole in the center and the keyed tabs on the ends match the orientation of the drawing. This habit ensures you’ll have them facing the right way when you begin assembly.
g.title: Leg and Spine Pieces

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint from the Components sheet for organizing the robot's short hardware.
The Visual: A white rounded rectangle on a blue background serves as a sorting area for "Type D" hardware. Inside is a silhouette of a very short screw with a measurement marker indicating a length of 1/4". Below the silhouette is a white square icon with the letter "D".
Text Prompt: "#4-40 Screw x 4".
Tactile & Mechanical Tips The "Stubby" Screws: These are the Type D screws, the shortest ones in your entire kit. At only 1/4" long, they are noticeably shorter than the Type A (3/8") and Type C (3/4") screws. Because they have fewer threads, they are easy to lose—placing them in this designated box immediately is a good move.
Quantity Check: You should have a total of 4 of these screws. These are primarily used to secure the spines to the torso and trunk. If you have any screws left over after filling your A and C boxes, they are likely Type D.
Screw Head Check: While all your #4-40 screws share the same diameter, the "D" screws are specifically chosen for shallow connections. If you accidentally use a Type A screw where a "D" should go, it might poke out too far and interfere with the robot's movement. Always double-check the length against the silhouette on this sheet before starting a new assembly step.
g.title: #4-40 1/4-inch screws

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint from the Components sheet for organizing the robot's secondary mechanical parts.
The Visual: A large black circle is centered on a blue background. The silhouette represents a flat, flexible ring.
Text Prompt: "O-Ring x 1" followed by the clarifying note "Approx. 2" in diameter."
Tactile & Mechanical Tips The "Elastic" Component: This is the large O-Ring. Unlike the other hardware, this part is flexible and stretchy. In the Walker robot, it is primarily used as a high-friction belt or a tensioner to hold the Ultrasonic Range Finder onto its wooden holder.
Diameter Check: As noted, this ring is approximately 2" in diameter. If your kit contains multiple rubber bands or rings, use the silhouette on this sheet as a 1:1 scale guide. Laying the O-Ring directly on the blue circle should show a perfect match around the edges.
Material Integrity: Inspect the surface of the O-Ring for any tiny cracks, dry-rotting, or "nicks." A healthy O-Ring should be smooth, shiny, and snap back to its original shape immediately after being stretched. If it feels brittle or shows white powdery residue, it may need a quick wipe with a damp cloth to restore its grip.
Storage Tip: Because this is the only O-Ring in the kit, keep it away from the sharp edges of the laser-cut plywood or the threads of the screws, which could puncture or weaken the rubber over time.
g.title: Rubber O-Ring

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint from the Components sheet showing the outline for the Range Finder Holder.
The Visual: A white-outlined silhouette of the Range Finder Holder is centered on a blue background. The part is a specialized plywood bracket with a wide, curved top edge to cradle the sensor, two keyed "T-shaped" tabs at the bottom, and two side-projecting tabs.
Text Prompt: "Range Finder Holder x 1".
Tactile & Mechanical Tips The "Head" Bracket: This piece acts as the neck and mount for the robot’s "eyes." When you lay it on the sheet, check the two cross-shaped tabs at the bottom. These must be perfectly intact, as they are the only points of contact that secure the entire sensor assembly to the robot's Torso.
Precision Fit: The wide, U-shaped cradle at the top is where the Ultrasonic Range Finder sits. Before moving to assembly, check the inner edges of this curve. If there is any leftover laser-cut "fuzz," it might prevent the sensor from sitting level, which can cause the robot to "look" at the floor instead of straight ahead.
The O-Ring Groove: You may notice small notches on the side tabs. These are designed to hold the O-Ring in place. Ensure these notches are clean so the rubber ring doesn't slip off while the robot is in motion.
Quantity Check: You should have exactly 1 of these holders. Because of its unique shape with the "head" and "feet," it is easy to distinguish from the flatter structural plates like the Torso or Trunk.
g.title: Wood Piece - Range Finder Holder

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint from the Components sheet for organizing the robot's primary actuators.
The Visual: A white rounded rectangle on a blue background serves as a designated sorting area for the FS90R Servo Motor. Inside is a detailed 3D isometric silhouette of a servo motor. It features a rectangular body with two mounting "ears" on the sides, each containing a small hole. On the top face, there is a circular output shaft (spline).
Text Prompt: "FS90R Servo Motor x 2".
Tactile & Mechanical Tips The "Muscle" of the Robot: You should have exactly 2 of these motors. When placing them on the sheet, make sure the wires (usually brown, red, and orange) are not pinched under the motor body so it can sit flat on the blueprint.
The Spline (Gear): The white or metal gear on top is the output spline. Be careful not to force-turn this by hand while the motor is off; doing so can damage the internal plastic gear train.
g.title: Electronic Components - Servo Motors

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint from the Components sheet showing the outline for the robot's trunk.
The Visual: A white-outlined silhouette of the Trunk is centered on a blue background. The trunk is a rectangular plywood plate with a circular tab on the left, a "T-shaped" cutout in the center flanked by two small screw holes, and two rectangular slots on the right edge. It also features etched branding text on the left side.
Text Prompt: "Trunk x 1".
Tactile & Mechanical Tips The "Rear Chassis": The Trunk is the rear section of the robot's main body. When laying this piece on the sheet, ensure the etched "PIPER" logo and website URL are facing upward. This ensures that when you move to assembly, the structural slots for the spines are oriented correctly.
Slot Check: This component has several critical mounting points. Before knolling it, run your finger over the two horizontal slots above and below the center cutout. These are where the Spines will eventually sit. Ensure they are clear of any debris so the keyed tabs of the spines can slide in without force.
g.title: Wood Piece - Trunk

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint from the Components sheet showing the outline for the robot's torso.
The Visual: A white-outlined silhouette of the Torso is centered on a blue background. It is a large rectangular plywood plate with rounded corners. The design features a large central "T-shaped" cutout, four horizontal slots (two above and two below the center), and a complex series of notches and a small square cutout on the left and right edges for mounting the head and trunk.
Text Prompt: "Torso x 1".
Tactile & Mechanical Tips The "Center Chassis": The Torso is the largest single piece of the Walker and serves as the main hub where the motors, legs (via the spines), and sensor are all anchored. When placing this on your sheet, look for the central "T" cutout—this is where the range finder assembly eventually sits.
Symmetry and Slots: You’ll notice two pairs of parallel horizontal slots. These are the primary connection points for the Spines. Just like with the Trunk, ensure these are clear of any tiny wood fragments (over-burn from the laser cutter) so that the spines can seat fully flush against the Torso.
g.title: Wood Piece - Torso

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint from the Components sheet for organizing the robot's primary electronic sensor.
The Visual: A white rounded rectangle on a blue background serves as a designated sorting area for the Ultrasonic Range Finder. Inside is a 3D isometric silhouette of the sensor, featuring two prominent cylindrical "eyes" (the ultrasonic transducers) mounted on a rectangular circuit board with a connector port at the top.
Text Prompt: "Ultrasonic Range Finder x 1".
Tactile & Mechanical Tips The Robot's "Eyes": You should have exactly 1 Ultrasonic Range Finder. These cylinders act as a speaker and a microphone to measure distance by bouncing sound waves off objects.
Handling the Circuitry: Unlike the wooden or metal parts, this is a sensitive electronic component. Avoid touching the gold-colored pins or the soldered connections on the back of the board, as static electricity or oils from your skin can interfere with its performance.
Connector Inspection: Look at the small plastic housing on the top of the sensor. Ensure the pins inside are straight and clear of any debris. When you eventually reassemble the robot, the jumper wires will plug directly into this port.
g.title: Electronic Component - Range Finder Module

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint from the Components sheet for the final servo hardware.
The Visual: A white rounded rectangle on a blue background serves as a designated sorting area. Inside is a silhouette of a very tiny screw with a standard Phillips head, angled diagonally. Below the silhouette is a white square icon with the letter "B".
Text Prompt: "Small Screws x 2".
Tactile & Mechanical Tips The "Spline Lock": These are the most critical screws for the robot's movement. They thread directly into the center of the FS90R Servo Motor shaft to keep the Spline Horn from flying off while the legs are rotating.
Magnetic Help: Because these are so incredibly small (and easy to lose), if you have a magnetized screwdriver, now is the time to use it. If not, placing them exactly on this blueprint spot will keep them from disappearing into the "void" of a messy desk.
Don't Over-tighten: The internal threads on the servo shaft can be delicate. When you eventually use these to lock the splines down, turn them until you feel firm resistance, then stop. You don't want to strip the motor's internal threading!
Quantity Note: You only need 2 of these because you only have two motors. If you find extra tiny screws in your kit (servos often come with a "spare" baggie), keep them in this box as backups.
g.title: Hardware - Small Screws (B)
